chunk_summary: A tiered counseling framework for adolescent supplement use emphasizes risk management, prioritizing whole foods over supplements, and addressing anti-doping risks in competitive athletes.
learning_objectives: Understand the tiered classification of supplements based on risk and medical necessity.
Learn counseling strategies for adolescent athletes regarding supplement use and dietary optimization.
Recognize the risks of high-performance products, including contamination and anti-doping violations.
key_facts: Tier 1 supplements address documented deficiencies (e.g., iron, vitamin D, folate, B12, zinc).
Tier 2 includes optional food substitutes for prolonged exercise but should not replace meals.
Tier 3 products (e.g., pre-workouts, fat burners) pose high risks of adulteration and medical harm.
National dietary guidance discourages reliance on protein supplements for muscle building in adolescents.
No supplement is 100% risk-free; contamination risks can lead to anti-doping violations.
reference_quote: National dietary guidance in India discourages reliance on protein supplements for muscle building and promotes diversified whole foods; this aligns with pediatric concerns that supplements displace meals and can reinforce disordered eating. (Source: Dietary Guidelines for Indians 2024, ICMR-NIN)
